most decent marketers, they understand the element of benefits. Right, they get that it's benefits, they understand the difference between features and benefits and so they understand that their marketing message needs to have benefits and be benefit driven and convey to the prospect the benefit that's in it for them, they get that. And a majority have these funnels that are just benefit laden, claim laden, promise driven and that's all they are. They're one claim after another after another after another, one promise after another, one benefit after another and there's no, nothing to convey believability. There's nothing that tells the prospect, yes this is legit, you can trust this, you can accept this, there's nothing that conveys to them, yes this makes sense. Right, see it's going back to that whole Aristotle but the core of influence, at the core of persuasion is both appealing to them emotionally, all the benefits, all the promises, all the claims but also making sure that your message resonates with them on a logical level. Right, and so that is where we need to prove the sales argument, we need to prove the idea, we need to lay out the idea to the point where prospects say that makes total sense and we do it using a technique that I really call the logic chain. So now here's how you begin this and Rich earlier alluded to this process slightly. So this starts by, and this is what I do all the time, this is what people pay me a lot of money to do for them. I always start out by asking what do these prospects need to believe to buy? Meaning, and now here's the thing that I want you to grasp. Right, so I said, I showed you earlier that we've got this timeline and I said that over here is the, this is the marketing and this is the selling. Right, and so what do they need to believe? Let me see if this is my luck like it won't erase or anything, it'll be like a regular permanent gig. I don't know if it is, so I'm just gonna do this. What do they need to believe at this point? What do they need to believe before I introduce them to my product or service? Before I begin my offer. What do they need to believe in order to buy? Right, in other words, when I say in order to buy, in order to be pre-sold, in order to want what it is that I'm about to offer them, what is it that they need to believe to buy? This is, like I said, the marketing. This point, from this point to this point, this is establishing those beliefs. In the marketing process, the marketing process, it's all about establishing beliefs. And so what beliefs? What do they need to believe to buy? Right, and so what that means is, let's break this down a little bit. That means is what do they need to believe about you? Right, and every product service offer is different. Like if they're working with you as a coach, if you were providing a coaching, if you were making a coaching offer, what they need to believe about you is very different from what they need to believe about you if they're buying a supplement. A lot less, that they need to believe about you if they were buying a supplement than if they were buying coaching. Do they need to believe that you have experience? Do they need to believe that you've been in the trenches? Do they need to belief that you understand where they've been? Do they need to believe that you've worked with people just like them? Do they need to believe that you're credentialed? Do they need to believe that you've got X number of years of experience? What do they need to believe about themselves? Do they need to believe that they've got the technical skills, that they've got the budget, that they've got the time, that they've got the know-how. Again, every product is different. Also, there are certain things that you could drill a little deeper with. Let's say you were selling a supplement, a fat burner, and somebody believes that they have a thyroid condition. If that's one of the things that they believe, that they have a thyroid condition, the odds on them buying your supplement in the end, if it doesn't address sluggish thyroid, is much, much lower. So in that case, one of the things that they might need to believe about themselves is that they do not have a thyroid condition. You see how some supplements, some offers, are going to change what it is that they need to believe. What do they need to believe about you? What do they need to believe about themselves? And what do they need to believe about your product or service? Your offer. Do they need to believe that it will pay for itself right away? Do they need to believe that it's easy? Do they need to believe that they have enough experience to understand it? Do they need to believe that they'll know how to use it? It's quick. It's easy that they've got the time. What do they need to believe as it relates to the offer? You've got to figure out, you've got to lay out what are the things that they need to believe to buy. And I'm going to break down an example for you, a specific example with a client in just a second. But remember when Rich was laying out up here? I forget exactly what he was saying, but he said they need to believe that this is their problem. They need to believe there is no other solution. They need to believe he is the best guy to give them the solution. Those are all things that they need to believe so that when he makes the offer, they are in a position to buy. And so just keep that in your mind as I continue to go through this. So presenting your sales argument. So what this is about is this is about selling the prospect emotionally and logically on your core idea. And when I say in this context, selling the prospect, I mean getting buy-in. Getting buy-in. If you're presenting an idea, like in Rich's case, let's say, going back to the whole, hey, look, your problem isn't that you don't know how to deal with all of these tactics and strategies and all these different things that you're doing and all these traffic things. Your problem is the fact that you're being an opportunity seeker when you should be a strategic entrepreneur. Well, you've got to create buy-in with that idea. That's what presenting the sales argument is about. It's about getting the prospect to the point where they say, I agree. Yes, I see that that's my problem now. I need to become a strategic entrepreneur if I want to fix this. And so that's what I'm going to do. That's what I mean when I say, sell them on your core idea. In Rich's case, it's core idea. And you'll see how I do this in just a bit. It's actually rather simple. That's what I mean when I say, sell them on your idea. Get that buy-in. Because no one's going to buy a course, a coaching program, on how to become a strategic entrepreneur if they haven't first bought into the idea that their problems stem from them not being a strategic entrepreneur. No one's going to buy a course on that if they don't first believe and accept and buy into that idea. And that is huge. That's huge. So this is why I said, it's what I call the flaw of pure benefits. Look, it's not about let me dump out as many benefits as I can. Let me make more and more promises to my prospect. That's not a sales argument. That's just you dumping out benefits and claims and promises. A sales argument is establishing the beliefs in their mind. And let me say this. This idea that there's a marketer out there. I just have to address this. I won't address them by name. But there is this idea that one marketer said, successful marketer, sharp marketer, said, look, you want to establish the least amount of beliefs as possible. You want to try to establish one, keep it to one belief. It's not about what you decide they need to believe. It's about what they need to believe to buy. This idea that, well, don't try to establish too many beliefs. It's not about, we're not arbitrarily saying, we're going to choose two beliefs for them to buy into. It's what do they need to believe to buy? That could be five. That could be 15. And it's not up to you to say arbitrarily, I'm going to leave one out. Because you'll see when I get to the example how, if you do leave one out, it could impact their belief on everything else. It could impact their entire acceptance of your message, of your sales argument. Erty said, nobody buys without belief. If they don't believe your premise, if they don't believe your idea, they're not buying. And so the higher the belief, the more belief you establish, the higher the response, the higher your conversions will be. How do we do this? We do this with what I call pre-selling education-based marketing, EBM content. James has had this drilled into him for, I don't even know how long now. And so education-based marketing, EBM content, I build every funnel on this. Every full-blown funnel that produces big money is always based on EBM content. So let me break this down. Education-based marketing. So education-based marketing is it's, I want to cover a couple of things. Number one, we are educating the prospect in a way. And you'll see this when I break down the example in a second. We're educating the prospects in a way that create the beliefs that they need to have. We're educating them so that logically, step by step, we create the beliefs in their mind that they need to have. All the way through to this point right here, when they have all the beliefs that they need, that's when introducing your product or service is easy. They're thankful when you introduce your product or service. Why? Because they believe what you need them to believe. And so we're not educating for the sheer sake of educating. Let me make sure that you are aware of that. There is this idea out there, especially for information marketers, for content-based marketers, that there was this concept at one point, pushing the free line. Push the free line. Who remembers that? You remember that? Push the free line. Is that like, who was that? That was not important. Who said that? All right? Right. But there was this concept, right? Like push the free line, which was, I'm going to give away better and better content. And the premise was, this idea that, well, if I give away enough great content, people are just naturally going to want to buy my stuff. That's not what I'm talking about here. I'm not advocating that at all. In fact, I'm advocating the opposite of that. What I mean is that it's education that is strategically designed to further the sale. When you are designing a marketing funnel, everything you do, everything you say, when you say it, where you say it, how you say it, and why you say it, is there to further the sale. If it doesn't further the sale, it shouldn't be in there. Period. If it doesn't further the sale, if it's not furthering an aspect of the sale, if it's not establishing a belief, if it's not establishing credibility, if it's not doing the things that I'm going to share with you, then it shouldn't be in there. And so in this context is education that is building desire. It's education that is establishing the beliefs. And so let me show this to you. So I start out by identifying, what do they need to believe to buy? At the end of this, before I present my product, what do they need to believe? What beliefs do they need to have in order to buy? What I tell them from the start of the funnel to the point where I introduce the product, I introduce like I begin the offer, I begin the transition into the offer, what I tell them from here and here is education, is educational material, valuable content that methodically one at a time creates those beliefs in their mind. So the point where right here, before I've ever introduced a product, they have bought into the whole premise behind this product. And so when I then give them the opportunity to buy, they're grateful. I'll show you how we do that. This is in line with this. This is hysterical. This was a quote or not a quote. This was a thing that I saw that Gary did in a presentation. So Gary Benzavango, one of the greatest living copywriters. Like I said, guy who couldn't be beat for many, many, many years. He said, this is the five word master secret for beating clutter, opening the sale, and achieving breakthrough results. Want to know what it is? Who wants to know what it is? Yes? I was just curious. I didn't include it in this presentation. What he said was, make your advertising itself valuable. That's at the root of, see, the beauty of why, and try to get this across to you guys as best I can. The reason why people will go through the funnels that I design, that we design, that my even students design using this principle, the reason why people will go through those funnels and love it, be thanking us for the actual funnel itself, or marketing them and selling them, and they're buying and they're thanking us, is because it's valuable. It has the perception of value. Where is that value coming from? That value is coming from the EBM content, the education based marketing content. What is that education based marketing content doing? It's establishing the beliefs that they need to have before we introduce the product, before we roll out the offer. Does that make sense before I go on? Yes? Cool. This is one of the things, the easiest way to go about this is to think like a prosecutor. To think like a prosecutor. I love this because to me, in my mind, this was a shift when this first hit me. I think I came up with this, really. Probably not, but I think I did. No, but think like a prosecutor. Think like a prosecutor, and the job, if you were prosecuting somebody on trial for murder, God forbid, and you were the prosecutor, so look, by the end of this case, by the time you make your closing arguments, these people, the one belief that they need to have is that that dude's guilty, that he did it. Like he so did it. That's one of the beliefs that they need to have. And so then, but before you establish that belief, there are other beliefs that they might need to have in order to get to that point where they believe this guy's guilty. Maybe they might have to believe he was there. They might have to believe that he had his hand on the weapon, that he had a motive, that he had violent tendencies, that whatever. They might have to have a number of different beliefs. So what does the prosecutor do? What does the prosecutor do when preparing for this case and when presenting this case? What they do is they start out with, well, what does this jury need to believe? What do they need to believe? What are the things that they need to believe? And what is the logical order that they need to believe these things so that the evidence continues to mount? They start off with one thing. They start off with, and then they go to another thing, another belief and another belief. And how are they doing that? So you're saying to yourself, I get it. We've got to establish beliefs. So how do you establish beliefs throughout a marketing funnel? So it's with claims and proof. That's really, in essence, what it is. When I say a claim, like so, if you are selling, and this is the example that I'm going to give you, and this is a real example from a client, so if you are selling a Facebook advertising course for pizzeria owners. Facebook advertising course for pizzeria owners, right? And so you said that Facebook will get you a ton of, what are you calling, pizzeria people? Pizza people. People that like pizza. Right? Well, whatever you say, that's a claim. That's all it is. Like, see, remember I said earlier that the healthiest perspective for you to have is they're not going to believe you. That's a claim. That's all it is. Anybody could say that. I could say anything. I could make any claim. That's not marketing. Marketing is not just making claims. Marketing is establishing beliefs in their mind. And you establish beliefs by making a claim and backing it up with proof. And so that's why this is really what it looks like. It is all about this. You make a claim. You back it up with proof. You establish a belief. Then you go to the next belief. You make a claim. You back it up with proof. You establish a belief. All the way through to this guy right here. And so you are backing everything up with proof. Claim, proof, claim, proof, claim, proof. That's like, if you don't have proof, you don't make the claim. And so here, let me do this and show you guys. So if you look at it, so there's this client. Guy said, Facebook advertising course selling to pizzeria owners. So if you think about what are some of the things that they need to believe pizzeria owners in order to buy a course on Facebook advertising? Go ahead. They need to believe it is work for other pizza owners. OK. Cool. It's good. What else? They can do it. They can do it. What else? Facebook invert. We're customers. Beautiful. How about even before then? It might be like, let's say that they've got to believe that the internet is a viable advertising medium. Can't assume that they already believe that. They might not even recognize that it's a viable. Maybe they have to also believe that it's a viable advertising medium for pizzeria owners. Then, right, but that's not enough just establishing this belief that it works for pizzeria owners. Maybe they need to believe that their local prospects are online and are accessible. That in other words, that the internet's not just this global marketing medium, but that they can actually access their local prospects. Maybe they also need to believe that their local prospects and their geography and their local geography, they respond to restaurant offers. That these are people that are actually responding to restaurant offers. Maybe they then need to believe that, let's say, just fast forward for time purposes, that they need to believe that paid advertising online is better than the free stuff. So paid is better than Google Maps and search engine optimization. Then after that, they need to believe that Facebook is the best paid advertising medium, that it's better than Google AdWords and Bing and all these other options. Then maybe they need to believe that it's easy, that they could do it, that they have the budget to do it, and that'll give them a fast ROI. So how do you do this? So if I just said the internet is a viable advertising medium, and that's all I said, that's a claim. Anybody could say that. So you've got to back it up with proof. I have to prove that. Say then that it's a viable advertising medium for restaurants. I've got to prove that. I can't just throw that out there. See, you can't assume that just because you've said it, they're going to believe it. That's the mentality that marketers have. I'm going to throw that out there. I'm going to say it, and they're going to believe it. And they don't believe it. Because what they're looking for is proof. Now check this out. So I said to you guys, now look. All of this. So I open up. I have a webinar. I have a funnel that goes through and educates. Educates, it's valuable. I'm educating them on, look, I'm going to show you how to grow your pizza. I'm going to show you how to double your bottom line in the next six months. And here's we're going to leverage the internet, blah, blah, blah. And so we start off by showing it's a viable, look, you need to be aware that the internet is a viable medium for. And we show them, prove it. And it's working like gangbusters right now for restaurant offers online and specifically in your geographic area. And we prove it. Now the thing you've got to understand is why paid traffic is better for you as a pizzeria and more effective than free. And then we show them, prove them, we go into. And here's why Facebook is the choice. Why it's the best choice, the best option for you. And establish that and blah, blah, blah, ROI and easy. Now look, check this out. I didn't say one thing about the product. I didn't say anything about the Facebook advertising course. Everything that we've done up to this point is marketing. Now when I said earlier, marketing for selling. That's marketing. Because what happens at this point, at that point, is you know what the pizzeria owners say? Man, I see it. I get it. We've got to get started with Facebook advertising. Man, that's the way to go. It's not, I get it. I totally get it. At which point we transition from marketing to selling. And then it becomes easy. Then it is, and hey, that's why I'm excited to share with you that I put together a course called Facebook Advertising for Pizzeria Owners. Let me tell you what you get with it today. Boom. Done deal, right? It's no high pressure because they're bought in on the idea. They're sold on the idea already from the marketing. All this educational value, right? All this is educational value that you've given them. But all of this was EBM content. I wasn't just educating them on anything just for the sheer sake of educating them. I wasn't trying to impress them with my knowledge. I wasn't trying to push the free line. I was educating them because every one of these points was a new belief that they need to have so that over here they were desiring the product before I even introduced them to the product. And so, let's keep going. Proof said, so boring, but it is so valuable, right? It's like the most boring thing to talk about. But I cannot stress this enough to you that it's the most prized element. Today, more so than ever before, because of the skepticism of markets as a whole, proof is the most prized element. If you look at the number one thing that's lacking in the majority of marketing funnels across the board, it's proof. There's no proof to back up the majority of what it is that's being said. In some cases, there's zero proof. And this is what prospects are looking for. They're looking to see if you've got proof. They're looking to see if what you're saying is just a claim or if it's fact. What do they use to determine whether it's a claim or it's a fact? Is proof. They want to see it. They want to see it. See, understand that it's the proof elements, the proof points, that give them the warm and fuzzies about what you're saying. It's the proof points that give them the belief that they so want. They want you to give them proof. And you see how over here, the thing that I want you to grasp, and this is the thing that separates Agora as a whole or what Agora does from what the typical online marketer does. You see here, I don't need to load this up with hyperbole. I don't need to talk about the ferocious results that you're going to get an explosive growth from untold wealth over here. See, I don't need to do that. And that's why you don't look like, when you use this process, you don't look like a jabron, like the typical knucklehead. See how I work that in, Joe? You like that, right? No, but you get that? I'm not relying on hyperbole. And what does this do? What does this do? It stimulates both intellectually and emotionally, because they say, I get it. I see it. I understand. I believe it. He's right. He's right. That's why, even as an absurd of an idea as a Facebook advertising courts for pizzeria owners, it's why the thing converted like crazy. Because they had every belief that we wanted them to have. So you've got to prove everything beyond a reasonable doubt. Like I said, the healthiest perspective to have is they don't believe a word that you're saying. They don't believe they're looking for reasons to doubt you. They're hoping that you give them the proof. They want to see the proof, but they're looking for reasons to doubt you. That's why you've got to prove everything beyond a reasonable doubt. I already said, don't assume that they're going to believe you simply because you say it. That's amazing how many clients I have that come to me. I ask them. So you said x, y, z. I believe you. And they're stumped. Stumped. You can't be stumped, not as a marketer, not with that question. General types of proof. General. Because usually we can work in 12 different types of proof. Generally, different types of proof. You've got logical. That's like stats, figures, studies. All logical. Then you've got anecdotal. That's all stories, tales, and whatnot. Social proof, that's what I was talking about earlier when I was saying folks just like them, like somebody who's appear or they perceive to be appear. And then there's demonstrative, demonstrating. And this is from Claude Hopkins. Everybody read Claude Hopkins in here? Yeah? Well, all right. So no argument in the world can ever compare with one dramatic demonstration. Dramatic demonstration, when you can. And I'm going to show you how most of you can. Because what's typically when marketers, whenever I talk about the dramatic demonstration for proving what it is that you're saying, marketers will say, well, I don't have a physical product. I don't have a piece of software. I'll show you how you don't need a piece of software or a physical product and how you could still do a dramatic demonstration. Let me give you an example. So Otis Elevator, so at one point when the first elevator was developed, cables were snapping. So yeah, people were on an elevator and all of a sudden cables snapped. And that's all she wrote for them. And so Otis Elevator, the way they took the market by storm is they invented this thing where if the cables busted, that the elevator would stop. People wouldn't plunge to their death. And so they created this thing. So what is the best way to prove that when you're on an Otis Elevator, you are safe? Is it to say it? Is it to give studies? Is it no? It's to demonstrate. And so what they did in the public square was here's the Otis Elevator, cut the cables and watch what happens. And it was because of that public demonstration that they took the market by storm. Profit hacks. We ran a promotion years ago. This was after when I came back in as a consultant for strategic profits, where Rich partnered up with a gentleman named Pete Williams. And Pete had this method, this system, for creating a week's worth of content that could be distributed to news release sites and blogs and videos sites and a podcast. And he had this methodology that he claimed, give me one hour, and I'll show you how to create a week's worth of content. And so we did a live stream. So we set up this live stream in the studio at strategic profits. And what did we do? We said, what you're going to see today is you are going to see live on this stream. Pete Williams create a week's worth of content in less than an hour. And so it was start the clock. Pete do his thing. Of course, behind the scenes there were like seven people that were working to get it done. But the reality is that that was part of his process. At the end of the hour, right? End of the hour, later, we were able to show there's the content, there's the interview, there's the podcast, there's the video that was created, there is all this content that was done. It was a dramatic demonstration. It was doing it live. And there's this, you know, the Timex, one of the old, when waterproof watches first hit the scene, what Timex did was they had this commercial where they attached the watches, the waterproof watches, to the backs of turtles and let them go deep. And then they showed how the watch was still working. That's a dramatic demonstration. There ain't nothing you need to say about that beyond that than showing that. There was a great, I have it posted somewhere, I couldn't find it on the MFA blog, of I think it was American Tourister. They had a commercial, you know, they had this commercial for like, their luggage is the toughest, it's a badass luggage that you could put through anything. Like, this is not going to get beat up, right? And so they didn't say that. What they did was they put like a gorilla in a cage with the luggage and you see the gorilla throwing the thing around and in the end the luggage is fine. That is a dramatic demonstration. This is an example of without a software, without software, without a physical product, how you can still have a dramatic demonstration, right? Like, do you make these mistakes in English? Like having valuable content in the actual promo that shows people you are making mistakes with your English, looking like an idiot, and I just proved it to you because here, I laid it out. Let me give you three examples. Do you make any of these mistakes? And people go through and they're like, oh, jeez, I do. Dramatic demonstration beyond just saying it. Right? I don't know if you ever saw there was one infomercial. I can't remember some of the names, but it was like some glove that you could put on and it would withstand anything. I forget, I totally forget the product now, but so they did this thing where they had Billy put on the glove and they rolled over his hand in a car. And he was like, fine, after that. And then he went and did some cocaine. And no, I'm kidding. But no, that's terrible. But that's a dramatic demonstration. You don't need to say much more than here. Look, who remembers the infomercial where the dude, it's like the new finish car wax where the guy is like, you put this on and you're going to be good to go. Don't worry about rain. Don't worry about mud. And so instead, hey, let's set the car on fire. And let's set the hood on fire. Set the hood on fire. The thing's burning. Wipe, put it out, wipe it away. The car is great. Still shiny, still phenomenal. Not much, again, you need to say. And then who's seen this one where the dude has the rubber in a can where he takes the bottom of a little boat? You seen that? He takes the bottom of a boat. He replaces the bottom of the boat, this little boat, with a screen door. Who's seen it? You guys haven't seen it yet? It looks good. Or whatever it is. I don't know. And the dude's like, you know, and then he sprays this stuff on this screen door, puts the boat in the water, and gets in. Now the thing that is amazing about, and you could see the dramatic demonstration in so many infomercials. There was the one with the glue where Billy was pulling a gigantic boat with. One of the things with the dramatic demonstration is when you are giving an example that's well beyond the need of the prospect. When it's something that the prospect will never have a need for. Because what it triggers in the prospect's mind is, oh, if it does that, then it's certainly going to take care of my needs. Like no one's thinking, hey, I'm looking for wax where I could set my car on fire. And no, it's like this is going to be effective. This stuff, this rubber in a can, is obviously going to be good enough for me to seal pots and pans or whatever. So if it works to do this, so it's beyond their need. Let me see this. The power of reason why. So again, you can't talk about proof without talking about reason why. So I'm going to give you examples of something. So earlier we talked about the unique mechanism. Also, one of the most valuable things that you can really understand. What is the unique part, piece, component of your product or service that delivers the end result? If we created an auto responder service that was going to compete with Aweber, and we made a claim like, hey, you get better deliverability with us, well, you got to give a reason why. The reason why is typically the mechanism. So you've got this. What slice, a better tasting soft drink. What is that? That's a claim. It's backed by no proof, and there's no reason why. So when you say, my product is better, my product functions better, and you're not giving a reason why, there's no proof. But this is the actual statement. Slice is better tasting soft drink because it contains 10% fruit juice, because gives the reason why. What is this 10% fruit juice that it contains? That's the unique mechanism. See how this is slight, a better tasting soft drink claim. Reason why, because it contains 10% fruit juice, that's the proof. What is that proof? The unique mechanism. Here, Kleenex towels absorb 50%. Like, absorb 50% more, that's really what I should have said, absorb 50% more. That would be a claim. If it was right here, Kleenex absorbs 50% more, that's a claim. You guys see that? I know I've said that, I've drilled that in. That's just a claim. There ain't nothing special about that. That's not going to be believed. But Kleenex towels absorb 50% more because, reason why, mechanism, they're two layers thick. That's the mechanism. That's the unique part of the Kleenex towel that delivers these additional results. See how that works? Power of candor. Sometimes, it's unbelievable how admitting a flaw, what's often referred to as the damaging admission. Candor has an impact on prospects. This was a famous ad where men wanted for hazardous journey, small wages, bitter cold, long months of complete darkness, constant danger, safe return, doubtful, honor, and recognition in case of success. They got flooded with responses. No joke. No freaking joke. That's right. You would think this is the complete opposite of what everybody would say. This is not like, let's spin it and say it's pretty decent wages. But no, it's the value of oftentimes candor. I want to go through this very quickly, and then I'm going to finish up by explaining and breaking down one funnel, the funnel that I've talked about. So this is generally, these are general. I'm sharing this because I think that this might be helpful for many of you. This is what Lawton referred to earlier when he said, Dan Kennedy's progressive sequence of agreements. And so this is about like, this goes back to this. There are certain beliefs, agreements, is what Dan refers to them as, that they need to have. Now Dan gives you general, like generally. And you're going to see that there is crossover here between what I shared with you and what Dan lays out. I just want to share this with you because I'm hoping that this will help really drive home this element. So here are the nine. And these are the nine agreements, the nine kind of things that each prospect, that prospects need to agree to. So number one is that the possibility. That just means the possibility of the promise. Like that it's possible that, yes, I can get more pizzeria people from the internet. Like the possibility. Then they've got to agree to the beneficial nature. They've got to agree that, yes, this would be beneficial for my pizzeria if this in fact was, right? And this is typically where like, you know, like this whole beneficial nature, this is where most marketers focus their attention. Actually, let me go through, I think I have it. Yeah, so possibility, don't assume that they agree and believe that it's possible. Just because you're saying something doesn't mean that they necessarily believe it. So that's one of those things. That's why I said, right, you can't take for granted that is the internet even a feasible medium for acquiring new patrons in your restaurant and your pizzeria. The other thing that I forgot to mention was, remember I said, like if you skip a belief, if you leave one out, you could impact the whole acceptance of your message. Well, imagine over here, right? Imagine over here with what I said with the whole pizzeria, they're accessible locally, they're responding to restaurant offers, paid traffic versus free traffic, Facebook versus all the others, blah, blah, blah. Well, imagine if we didn't say anything or do anything to establish the belief that paid traffic is better than free traffic for pizza reowners. Let's say we left that out altogether. We totally left this bad boy out. Well, you see, what happens is you arrive in the end with not necessarily them having the full belief that you need because now they could be thinking in their mind, well, why would I do Facebook advertising when I can get tons of people from free listings? Like I heard you could be listed on Google and get a whole bunch of free traffic. So why would I pay for advertising? I don't get it. See, because they don't have the belief. That's why you can't skip one of these beliefs. You can't leave these out. You've got to make sure that you have a solid logic chain, a solid argument, a solid case. Think like a prosecutor. Beneficial nature. And the beneficial nature, this is the subtle point. Each one of these is a subtle point that I wanted you to be aware of underneath. Beneficial nature. That's common sense for us in this room as marketers. But here's the thing, that that benefit must be sufficient to move action. So what that means is like, so if the Agora guys were creating a promotion and a big idea of this, that, and it was built around the fact that you could increase your money by 1.5%, not really sufficient. Not sufficient. Beneficial, yes. But not sufficiently beneficial. Not enough. So you've got to make sure that it's sufficient. Attainability. So what is this about? So what's the difference between something being attainable and something being possible? Well, the difference is in the practicality compared to the feasibility or the possibility. Right now it's like, well, is it practical? Is it practical for me to do that? Because look, is it possible that you could turn a dollar into $5 million in one week? Sure. You could win the lottery. It's possible. Is it practical? It's different. So it's one thing to prove and get acceptance and agreement on something being possible. It's another thing for them to believe that this is truly attainable, that it's practically attainable, reasonably attainable. Then there is personally attainable. That they agree that it's attainable for them. And so you do that with typically diversity and preponderance of testimonials. And the way you do this is with a variety of testimonials. Like in other words, you want to get a diverse group of testimonials so that you can demonstrate that it is effective, that it's personally attainable for folks in all kinds of situations. And the volume of testimonials also understand that if you're selling to older individuals, to the elderly, obviously it goes without saying that the more testimonials that you have from elderly folks, the more believable it's going to be. Just having one testimonial from an old woman isn't necessarily going to be sufficient to convince the elderly that it's personally attainable for them. Understand that fear of failure, fear of failure, and the perceived associated criticism that folks are going to get, is a stronger motivator, is a stronger emotion than the possibility of gain. That's a valuable, extremely valuable nugget for each and every one of you. What that means, that's like this. Like the fear of failure, think about it this way. So let's say you said to a group of 100 individuals that if you don't take XYZ action, if you take XYZ action, if you do XYZ, you'll get $100 deposited in your bank account at the end of the day. If you do XYZ, whatever XYZ is, you'll have $100 deposited in your bank at the end of the day. Or you said to a different group of 100, if you do not do XYZ, $100 will be withdrawn from your bank. Which one is going to move more people to action? What was that? The loss. Understand, right? Understand what's more of a motivator. What's more of a motivator? Same activity, same exact activity. We're still asking them to do XYZ. Say you do it, and you'll get this. You don't do it, you'll lose this. People that we propose to lose this. This was, right? So earlier Rich mentioned the whole, it's not your fault. Like the common in copy, it's not your fault. You've been lied to. It's not your fault. They've kept the secrets from you. What I'm talking about in this context is that you've got to show the prospect that it's not them, like Rich said, that it's the mechanism, the system, the process that they've used. They haven't made money from email marketing, not because of email marketing doesn't work, not because of them, because they've used the wrong email marketing system. They've used the wrong process. And so the way you do that is by showing how your solution is different from anything else. If you want them to feel like, ah, that's why this could work, and that's why nothing has worked up to this point, it hasn't worked up to this point, because the system, and this is vastly different from anything else that I've used before. This is profoundly different. This is the unique mechanism. This is the unique mechanism. And I hope you see how this stuff is not that difficult when you understand it. This stuff is not that difficult. Like I was saying, if I was marketing an email autoresponder service, and we wanted to talk about the reason why your email hasn't worked, your deliverability sucks from the prior company that you've been using, because they don't have the XYZ technology that we have. They don't have the process for making sure that all the IPs are whitelisted. They don't have, right? Showing that's why. That's why. That's why this time is different, because the system is different. Before it wasn't your fault, it wasn't you, it was that. But now you have this. And this, the unique mechanism, it's going to give it to you. And so personal beneficial nature, this is why does taking the action make sense to them? This is not just about, is this beneficial? This is about beneficial to them. Now you're making it personal. So we talked about possible, attainable, personally attainable. Because again, understand for the prospect, very feasible, very possible for them to say this is attainable, not attainable by me. Sure, I get the fact that it's possible. I get the fact that other people have done it. I can't do it. You don't create the agreement that they can do it. You don't get them to buy into that they can do it done. Now you leave it out there for, I hope that they believe that they can do it. Advantages are urgent timing. This is just about exactly what it said. Why is now the time? And we all know that when it comes to marketing and selling, right, that it's about now, giving them a reason to respond now. Why is now the right time? That reason why now is a good time. Could be scarcity, it could be urgency, it could be ideal timing. In other words, in financial promos, it's often a lot easier. Something's about to happen. Get in now, and you'll get rich. Advantages timing. Urgent timing is when there's a specific deadline. Remember, I showed you that promotion on December 31st. XYZ is going to happen. Well, that's urgent timing. Then there's this whole idea of personal. See, there's a difference between something being advantageous generally for the general market and for a prospect to understand why it is personally advantageous for them to respond now. See, you have to, in this case, you have to, and these are, these little guys down here are more my commentary on this, you've got to overcome the reasons why the prospect might think now is not the ideal time. Why don't they have to do it now? Cool, that sounds great. I believe it. It's possible. It's attainable. I see why it's beneficial for me. Possible for me, attainable for me. I get all that, but you know what? I think I'm going to wait until I have a little bit more money. That's right, allowing a reason to be out there that makes it not necessarily advantageous or urgent timing for them right now. You've got to get them to agree that now is advantageous for them. There's a benefit. There's an advantage for them responding now. And then just personal appropriateness. And this is not always, you don't always have to get into this in many niches, but depending on the niche, depending on the market, there might be, is this morally appropriate, does this line up with their values and whatnot? And so this is just, in some cases, it's showing them that, yes, this is appropriate for you. This, you're not going to have to repent or anything. This is all good. And so it makes sense. You guys cool with that? So my final slide. This is it. This is all she wrote. So this is a funnel. These are screenshots from a funnel that has been cranking, generates new customers every single day from cold traffic and has been for months and months. And it actually was first done live as our launch. Actually, Andre was our partner who drove the most sales, which is not typical for him, but, I'm just kidding. It's so is. It's ridiculous. That's, I learned. Let me tell you this. We'll edit this out later. But, so I was, so I participated in the first launch that I ever participated in as a partner for, you know about Brian and Scott? For Brian and Scott, right, of Get 10,000 Fans. And so a couple of things that, so I never participated in a launch. I'm not a huge fan of participating in launches. I shared that, I think, a little bit last night. And the funny thing, there's two things that I learned from that launch experience. One was the enormous power of prizes, because it was no longer about the money for me, like, you know, the commission or whatever that we were getting paid. There was this scooter that like he was giving away, which was like, I don't even know, a $2,500 scooter or whatnot. And, you know, I just wanted this scooter so freaking bad. I was literally going on there at points refreshing my stats, like, it was absurd. So I learned the power of prizes and how it's amazing. And the leaderboard, just like, you know, disgusted with myself for dropping down. The other thing that I learned was that, and I said this publicly, was that I never ever wanted to compete against Andre again. Because I was like, while he was emailing, I don't even know, a thousand people or something, I was like, ding in my credit card on traffic, every kind of traffic technique you could think of. I was like, you know, and the team was like, what are you doing? I'm like, I don't care. Just drive, freaking traffic. I don't care. I do not care. We can't even track it. I don't care. I don't care. I couldn't, for the life of me, I couldn't do anything to even come remotely close. And then the final day, it was like, thoroughly embarrassing. Like, I shut it down. I was like, I'm not even checking anymore. Like, yeah, and then I got an email from Brian running the launch. And the funny thing was the whole time, you know, Brian was such a good motivator, cause he would always be like, he would be cryptic. He'd be like, yeah, man, you're close. You're close. Like, dude, you don't understand. You're on his tail. And then like, you know, could have been like six hours remaining, he was like, yeah, you're not even close. Yeah, yeah, it's not happening for you. You're not even close. Thanks for your support. And I was like, son of a... Anyway, this is from the funnel that we launched publicly back in March. And then with partner support and an hour little internal list at the time, and it did pretty well. It did really well for us. People loved it. And then video four was basically the selling. So first of all, bringing this back full circle to you. Bringing this back full circle to you. So I already mentioned that four videos, the first three videos, so 75%, didn't mention a word about the product. There was no product. There was no product features. There was no product benefits. There was no product advantages. There were no testimonials for the product. There were none of that. In the first three videos, 75% of the content, there was none of that. What was the first 75% of the content doing? Video one, video two, video three? It was establishing the beliefs in their mind that I needed them to believe so that when I offered them the six figure funnel formula course, they snatched it up. So let me tell you a little bit about what I had to establish. So first of all, in video one, video one, breaking down these videos. I break them down like this. And you'll see how this all ties together. Video one is really the what and the why now. Meaning, so what are we talking about? What we're talking about marketing funnels. And so why? Why now? Why do you need to pay attention to marketing funnels? Why do you need to? And you'll see it's the same process, same process here. So in this, it was me selling them on the idea that your problem is not a lack of traffic. Your problem is the fact that your problem isn't like you got necessarily a crappy product or that. The problem is you don't have a marketing funnel in place to convert your traffic. The problem is not a traffic issue. It's a conversion issue. See, I had to get that buy in. That's one of the beliefs that they needed to have. If they didn't believe that they had a conversion problem and I allowed them to continue to believe that it was a lack of traffic and that more traffic would solve that for them, then they're going to be looking for a traffic solution. But so I had to show them, no, that's not your problem. It's not a lack of traffic. And I did that by showing them that there's more traffic opportunities today than ever before. There's no lack of traffic. You want to get traffic. You could send traffic. The problem is that most marketers can't afford to invest in traffic because they don't have a way to convert that traffic into money and get the money back. For the majority of marketers, they can't afford to put a dollar into traffic because they only get $0.20 back. But the game changes for you when you could put a dollar in and get at least $1 back on the front end. And so I went through and I established that in video one. And I proved it beyond a shadow of a doubt. And so then I, right? So at the end of this, I talked about, so OK. So now you recognize that you need a funnel. So here is what most marketers do. Most marketers do what's called a Frankenstein funnel. And so I gave it a name for something. I named an issue in the marketplace. And I said, a Frankenstein funnel is when you'll look at one marketers funnel and say, oh, that's a great squeeze page. That's a great upsell page. That's a great sales page. And then you'll piece it together. And so I said, you end up with a Frankenstein funnel. And so what is that like? Remember I said, that's like what would happen if you took the best component from different luxury car brands? If you took the best component, the electronic system from BMW, you took the engine from Ferrari, you took the tires from an Aston Martin, whatever. You took what would happen? You'd wind up with a car that doesn't run. You can't piecemeal your funnel together, right? So see how? Ah, I got it. I see what you're saying. So then in video two, I lay out, this is the process that you need to follow. These are the five steps of the six-figure funnel formula. What's the six-figure funnel formula? The unique mechanism. And so right now, let me also say, and I mentioned earlier, I think it was when Rich was here, right? That you're better off almost always. You're better off repositioning what it is that you're providing as a new thing, as a new opportunity, not improvement. I didn't say, here's how to improve your squeeze page. Here's how to improve your conversions. Here's how to make a better offer. I didn't say that. See, they don't want improvement. They want new. This is a new system. This is different. And so what I did was I showed that the five main pieces. So I laid it out. I said, here's the six-figure funnel formula. Here it is. Here are the five main pieces of this process. Let me show you. Then I actually showed them how easy it is to get started with traffic. Why? Pages up, live, right there. There you go. What did I do? Established a belief in their mind that it's easy, that they didn't need a webmaster, that they didn't need a lot of tools, that it could be done very quickly. And I did it with a, I demonstrated it. And then, of course, in the final video was the sales video. The video just was like, let me tell you about the six figure funnel formula. And really, at the end of video three, I said something like, now look, I'm sure you're smart enough to realize that this is leading up to an opportunity for you to take part in a program. And so in the next video, I'm going to tell you all about the six figure funnel formula training program. And then that's what I did. But do you see how up to this point, this was all marketing? There's no selling, no, none of that. It's this video where I transition into, here's what I've got. This is everything that you're going to get. These are the features. These are the benefits. These are the advantages. This is what you're going to get. And just as to wrap this baby up, I'll tell you that. What made this thing stellar? Especially during the live launch was we created the perception of value that was so much higher than the price point that we rolled with. So in other words, we created the response in prospects that their anticipation was the price point was going to be much higher. And so when we rolled with the price point, when we dropped, when we showed this is everything you get, and this is the price point, we had done such a good job of building up value that they were floored. There are videos from clients that talk about, there was one video from a client that was just submitted the other day where he said something like he said, I was watching the launch because I wanted to learn, study, see what you were doing. I just dropped $1,000 on another program. I had already decided no way was I buying anything else. I watched your videos. And then when you dropped the offer, he was like my mouth, my jaw hit the floor, and I had no other option but to respond. And I was like, sweet. That's what we wanted.
